background_information.other_where_you_saw_opportunity: I first came across this volunteer opportunity while researching long-term care volunteer programs in Calgary. I was specifically looking for organizations that work with seniors and individuals with complex care needs. A Google search for "Calgary long term care volunteering" brought up the Carewest website. I spent about an hour reading through the various Carewest site descriptions, volunteer role descriptions, and the philosophy of care. I was impressed by the focus on person-centered care and the range of therapeutic recreation programs offered. After reviewing the information, I bookmarked the site and came back a few days later to complete this application.

experience.experience_description:
I am a 37-year-old administrative professional currently working full-time as a Client Services Coordinator at a mid-sized accounting firm in downtown Calgary. I have been in this role for the past five years, and my day-to-day responsibilities include front desk reception, managing a multi-line phone system, greeting and assisting clients (many of whom are older adults), scheduling appointments, organizing paper and electronic files, and providing general administrative support to a team of ten accountants.

Prior to my current position, I worked for three years as an administrative assistant at a family medical clinic in the southwest of Calgary. In that role, I became very comfortable interacting with patients of all ages, including many seniors and individuals with mobility challenges or chronic health conditions. I regularly helped patients complete intake forms, coordinated with nurses and physicians, and became familiar with maintaining confidentiality and following clinic protocols. This experience helped me develop strong communication skills, patience, and the ability to explain information clearly and calmly, especially to individuals who might feel anxious or overwhelmed.

In terms of volunteer experience, I have been involved in community and helping roles for most of my adult life:

1. Calgary Public Library – Reading Buddies Program (2018–2022)
   - I volunteered twice a month at the Central Library as a Reading Buddy, working one-on-one with children in Grades 1–3 who were struggling with reading. While this program was focused on children rather than seniors, it taught me how to adapt communication styles, use encouragement effectively, and structure short activities to keep someone engaged. I often prepared simple reading games or brought in age-appropriate books and asked the children to choose what interested them. This helped me understand how to balance guidance with letting the other person feel in control of the interaction.

2. Community Seniors Outreach – Friendly Phone Calls (2020–2021)
   - During the early part of the COVID-19 pandemic, I volunteered with a local seniors outreach initiative organized through my community association. I was paired with three seniors who were largely homebound and feeling isolated. I called each of them once a week, usually for about 30–45 minutes, to check in, offer companionship, and help them problem-solve small challenges (like arranging grocery deliveries or explaining how to use video calling apps). One of the women I called, an 82-year-old widow named Margaret, often shared stories about her time as a nurse and her life raising a family in Calgary. Over time, I learned how important consistency and reliability are—she would often say she looked forward to my call all week. This experience reinforced my interest in working with older adults and showed me how meaningful even small acts of connection can be.

3. Church-based Meal Program – Occasional Volunteer (2016–2019)
   - Through a local church, I helped prepare and serve community meals about once a month. Many of the attendees were low-income seniors or individuals experiencing housing instability. My tasks included setting tables, plating food, serving coffee and tea, washing dishes, and chatting with guests. I learned how to work as part of a team in a busy environment, follow food safety guidelines, and be sensitive to the dignity and autonomy of each person—always asking before assisting, offering choices, and respecting when someone preferred to sit quietly.

4. Office Wellness and Social Committee (2019–Present)
   - Within my current workplace, I co-chair our Wellness and Social Committee. While this is not traditional community volunteering, it has given me experience planning events, coordinating schedules, and organizing group activities. For example, I helped coordinate a holiday donation drive for a local seniors’ home, where we collected blankets, puzzle books, and toiletries. I handled communication with the facility, created sign-up sheets, and managed the drop-off logistics. This experience has made me comfortable with planning and following through on commitments, which I know will be important in a structured volunteer role with Carewest.

Skills and strengths relevant to volunteering at Carewest:
- Strong interpersonal and communication skills: I am comfortable initiating conversations, listening attentively, and adapting my communication style to the person I’m with. I try to be genuinely curious about people’s stories and experiences.
- Reliability and consistency: In both my work and volunteer roles, I have a strong track record of showing up on time, following schedules, and communicating clearly if something needs to change. I understand that residents and staff depend on volunteers.
- Comfort with seniors and diverse needs: Through my work at the medical clinic and my seniors outreach volunteering, I have become comfortable interacting with people who may have mobility issues, hearing or vision impairments, or cognitive changes. I do my best to speak clearly, face the person when talking, and check for understanding without being patronizing.
- Organizational skills: I am detail-oriented and like to prepare in advance. For example, if I were assisting with recreation activities, I would be comfortable helping set up the room, gather supplies, and make sure everything is put away properly afterwards.
- Calm and patient demeanor: Co-workers and friends often describe me as calm and steady. I rarely get flustered and am able to stay patient even if someone repeats themselves or needs extra time.

Reasons for wanting to volunteer at Carewest:
I am specifically interested in volunteering with seniors in a continuing care or supportive living environment because I have seen in my own family how important social connection and meaningful activities are for quality of life. My grandmother lived in a long-term care facility in Edmonton for the last six years of her life. I visited as often as I could, and I noticed how her face would light up when volunteers came in to play music, run a bingo game, or simply sit and talk. Some of her closest relationships in those final years were with staff and volunteers. That experience stayed with me, and now that my own schedule is more stable, I would like to offer that same kind of support to others.

I am hoping to be involved in roles such as visiting with residents one-on-one, assisting with group recreation programs (like crafts, games, or music), helping with outings if appropriate, or supporting special events. I am open to training and feedback and understand that there are policies and boundaries that volunteers must follow, especially related to privacy, safety, and infection control.

Overall, I see this volunteer role as a long-term commitment rather than a short-term requirement. I would like to start with one consistent weekly shift and, once I am comfortable and established, potentially increase my involvement if there is a need. I am motivated by a genuine desire to contribute to residents’ emotional and social well-being, and I am very willing to learn from Carewest staff about how I can be most helpful.
